_meta.authoringNotes: Epidemiology and pathophysiology from Pearce EN 2003 PMID 12699500, Yu & Bhatt 2009. Microbiology of acute suppurative thyroiditis from Brook I 2010 PMID 20593248. Pyriform sinus fistula data from Miyauchi et al. 1991. Radiation thyroiditis from Hancock et al. 2003.

h1Title: Acute Thyroiditis: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Acute thyroiditis is the rarest form of thyroid inflammation, accounting for fewer than 1% of all thyroid disease, and is characterized by rapid onset of anterior neck pain, fever, and swelling of the thyroid gland caused by bacterial or, less commonly, fungal or parasitic infection. In children, a congenital pyriform sinus fistula provides a direct bacterial entry route into the left thyroid lobe, making recurrent acute suppurative thyroiditis in children an almost pathognomonic presentation. In adults, acute thyroiditis most often arises from hematogenous bacterial spread in immunocompromised patients. Without prompt surgical drainage and targeted antibiotic therapy, a thyroid abscess can rupture into the mediastinum or compress the airway. Radiation thyroiditis is a distinct, usually self-limiting acute form caused by radioactive iodine therapy or external beam radiation.

keyStats.prevalence: Fewer than 1% of all thyroid disease; estimated incidence below 0.5-1 case per 100,000 per year in population-based series; exact epidemiology limited by rarity
keyStats.demographics: Children under 10 predominate in pyriform-sinus-fistula-associated cases, with a marked left-sided thyroid lobe predilection (over 90%); immunocompromised adults (HIV, transplant, malignancy) represent the largest adult subgroup
keyStats.avgAge: No dominant peak age; children 3-10 years for congenital fistula forms; adults of all ages in hematogenous forms; radiation thyroiditis follows radioactive iodine therapy at any adult age
keyStats.globalCases: Exact global figure not established; reported predominantly from Japan, the United States, and India in case series; likely globally underdiagnosed in resource-limited settings

definition:
Acute thyroiditis (ICD-10: E06.0), also called acute suppurative thyroiditis, acute infectious thyroiditis, or acute bacterial thyroiditis, is an uncommon inflammatory condition of the thyroid gland caused by direct bacterial infection, producing suppuration, abscess formation, and rapid tender enlargement of part or all of the thyroid gland. Unlike autoimmune or granulomatous forms of thyroiditis, acute thyroiditis results from microbial invasion by hematogenous spread, direct spread from adjacent neck structures, external trauma, or — particularly in children — passage of bacteria through a congenital pyriform sinus fistula.

The thyroid gland is inherently resistant to infection because of its rich blood supply, high iodine content (which is bactericidal), encapsulation, and lymphatic drainage; these factors explain the rarity of acute thyroiditis despite the prevalence of neck infections. The most common causative organisms are Staphylococcus aureus, Streptococcus pyogenes, Streptococcus pneumoniae, anaerobes (especially in dental-origin spread), and Gram-negative rods in immunocompromised patients. Atypical pathogens — Aspergillus, Candida, Pneumocystis, and mycobacteria — are seen in severely immunocompromised individuals.

A distinct clinical form — radiation thyroiditis — follows radioactive iodine-131 treatment for hyperthyroidism or thyroid cancer, typically appearing 5-10 days post-dose with transient thyroid swelling and pain. This form is not infectious but represents acute inflammatory cell infiltration following radiation injury. Management is led by endocrinologists, head and neck surgeons, and in children, pediatric surgeons for fistula repair.

typesClassification.description: The dominant form. Bacterial infection produces acute abscess within the thyroid. In children, a pyriform sinus fistula (left-sided in over 90%) provides a direct anatomical pathway. In adults, hematogenous spread from bacteremia, IV drug use, or dental source predominates. Requires antibiotics and surgical drainage.

A congenital anomaly from incomplete third or fourth pharyngeal pouch obliteration creates a fistula from the pyriform sinus to the thyroid or adjacent tissues. Recurrent left-sided acute thyroiditis in a child under 10 is the hallmark presentation. Definitive treatment is surgical excision of the fistula tract after resolution of the acute episode.

Acute inflammatory reaction following radioactive iodine-131 (I-131) therapy, typically appearing 5-14 days after dose. Self-limiting over 2-4 weeks; managed with NSAIDs or short-course oral corticosteroids. Rarely, transient thyrotoxicosis can occur from follicle disruption releasing stored thyroid hormone. Does not require antibiotics.

Unusual organisms — Aspergillus, Pneumocystis jirovecii, Candida, mycobacteria — cause acute thyroiditis in patients with HIV-AIDS, hematologic malignancies, or bone marrow transplant. Clinical presentation may be insidious; diagnosis requires aspiration for culture and special stains.

primarySymptoms: Rapid onset of anterior neck pain, often severe, that worsens with swallowing, head movement, or direct palpation over the thyroid gland — typically the most prominent presenting symptom.

Unilateral or bilateral tender, warm swelling of the thyroid gland with overlying skin erythema and warmth, distinguishing acute thyroiditis from the firm or nodular enlargement of goiter or malignancy.
Fever above 38°C (100.4°F) with chills, malaise, and systemic toxicity proportional to the extent of infection and the virulence of the causative organism.
Dysphagia (difficulty swallowing) and odynophagia (painful swallowing), sometimes accompanied by drooling in severe cases with peritonsillar or parapharyngeal involvement.
Dysphonia (voice change) or hoarseness from edema of adjacent laryngeal structures or direct compression of the recurrent laryngeal nerve by the inflamed thyroid or abscess.
Soft, fluctuant swelling over the thyroid that is detectable on palpation when an abscess has formed, particularly in the left thyroid lobe in children with pyriform sinus fistula.
Referred ear pain (otalgia) along the course of the superior laryngeal nerve, a common accompaniment to anterior neck infection and thyroid inflammation.
Transient symptoms of hyperthyroidism — palpitations, tremor, heat intolerance, sweating — in a minority of patients where follicle disruption releases stored thyroid hormone into the circulation.
earlyWarningSigns: Progressive, worsening anterior neck pain in a child with a history of prior episodes of left-sided thyroid swelling — a pattern highly suggestive of recurrent pyriform sinus fistula-related thyroiditis.
Fever and neck swelling in an immunocompromised adult (post-transplant, HIV, chemotherapy) without an obvious source — should prompt consideration of acute thyroid infection.
A tender enlarging thyroid nodule in the context of recent pharyngitis, dental abscess, or bacteremia — suggesting hematogenous seeding of a pre-existing thyroid nodule.
Mild dysphagia with anterior neck tenderness in a patient 5-14 days after radioactive iodine treatment — early radiation thyroiditis before full inflammatory response develops.
emergencySigns: Rapidly progressing neck swelling with stridor, accessory muscle use, or inability to lie flat — airway compression from a rapidly expanding thyroid abscess is a surgical emergency requiring immediate ENT or head and neck surgery evaluation.

Fever above 39.5°C with spreading neck erythema, crepitus on palpation, or tense cervical swelling — features suggesting deep-space infection, necrotizing cervical fasciitis, or mediastinal extension requiring immediate CT scanning and surgical consultation.

Hypotension, tachycardia, and altered mental status in a patient with fever and thyroid swelling — thyroid abscess sepsis with bacteremia requiring immediate IV antibiotics, blood cultures, and resuscitation.
Acute respiratory failure or inability to phonate after thyroid infection — posterior thyroid abscess extension compressing the trachea constitutes a life-threatening airway emergency.

causes.description: A persistent remnant of the third or fourth pharyngeal pouch creates a fistulous tract from the piriform sinus of the hypopharynx to the left thyroid lobe in over 90% of cases. Bacteria colonizing the pharynx gain direct access to the thyroid. This is the dominant cause of recurrent acute thyroiditis in children under 10 and can present multiple times before the fistula is identified and surgically excised.

Bacteremia from a distant focus — pneumonia, endocarditis, dental abscess, skin infection, or urinary tract infection — can seed the thyroid in immunocompromised individuals. The thyroid's natural resistance is compromised in HIV infection, organ transplantation, malignancy, long-term corticosteroid use, and diabetes mellitus, allowing lodgment and multiplication of circulating bacteria.

causes.cause: Direct extension from adjacent neck infection
causes.description: Ludwig's angina, peritonsillar abscess, retropharyngeal abscess, or deep cervical space infection can extend directly into the thyroid capsule through lymphatic or tissue-plane spread. Mixed flora including oral anaerobes (Fusobacterium, Prevotella), streptococci, and staphylococci predominate.

Fine-needle aspiration biopsy of the thyroid, central venous catheter placement, anterior cervical spine surgery, thyroid surgery complications, and external trauma introducing skin bacteria into thyroid tissue. Rare cause in clinical practice but documented in case series.

causes.cause: Radioactive iodine or external beam radiation
causes.description: Radioactive iodine-131 used for hyperthyroidism or differentiated thyroid cancer produces acute follicular necrosis and inflammatory infiltration 5-14 days post-dose. External beam radiation to the anterior neck for lymphoma or head and neck cancers can produce delayed acute or subacute radiation thyroiditis within weeks of treatment.

Aspergillus, Candida, Pneumocystis jirovecii, atypical mycobacteria, and other unusual organisms cause acute thyroiditis in patients with severely compromised immunity. Aspergillus thyroiditis has been reported in post-bone-marrow-transplant patients and carries a high mortality if not treated with systemic antifungals combined with surgical drainage.

riskFactors.factor: Congenital pyriform sinus fistula in children
riskFactors.description: Present in the majority of children with recurrent acute thyroiditis; the fistula provides a permanent bacterial entry route until surgically repaired.
Severe immune compromise is the dominant risk factor for acute thyroiditis in adults; the thyroid's intrinsic resistance is insufficient when innate and adaptive immunity are globally suppressed.

riskFactors.factor: Active bacteremia from a distant source
riskFactors.description: Untreated or inadequately treated bacteremia — dental abscess, pneumonia, endocarditis — provides the circulating bacterial inoculum for hematogenous thyroid seeding.
Thyroid nodules, cysts, or areas of pre-existing glandular damage may provide a nidus for bacterial infection with lower inoculum than required to infect normal thyroid tissue.

riskFactors.factor: Recent radioactive iodine therapy or neck irradiation
riskFactors.description: Radiation damage to thyroid follicles produces necrotic tissue and impaired local defenses; radiation thyroiditis is expected and manageable, but field irradiation also predisposes to secondary bacterial infection.
IVDU-associated bacteremia with skin flora (S. aureus, Gram-negative rods) provides a recurrent bacteremic exposure capable of seeding the thyroid in susceptible individuals.

affectedDemographics: Children aged 3-10 years: the leading demographic for pyriform sinus fistula-associated recurrent acute suppurative thyroiditis, with a striking left-sided predominance.
Immunocompromised adults: patients with HIV-AIDS, post-transplant immunosuppression, hematologic malignancy, or long-term corticosteroid use account for the majority of adult cases.
Adults with odontogenic infections: submandibular and cervical deep space infections from dental sources are a recognized entry pathway for mixed-flora thyroid abscess.
Post-radioactive iodine therapy patients: a common, expected, and generally mild form of radiation thyroiditis occurring in up to 30% of patients receiving I-131 therapy.
diagnosisOverview:
Acute suppurative thyroiditis is a clinical and imaging diagnosis confirmed by culture. The clinical triad of acute anterior neck pain, fever, and a tender thyroid swelling in a child with prior similar episodes should immediately trigger suspicion for pyriform sinus fistula-associated thyroiditis. In adults, the history must address immune status, recent infections, and possible entry portals.

Blood tests show leukocytosis with neutrophilia and elevated CRP and ESR, consistent with bacterial infection. Thyroid function tests are typically normal or show transient mild elevations of free T4 and T3 from follicle disruption, with TSH suppressed in the acute phase; frank hypothyroidism from gland destruction is rare but reported in severe disease.

Ultrasound is the first-line imaging modality: it identifies a hypoechoic or heterogeneous area of thyroid inflammation, confirms abscess formation, defines extent, and guides fine-needle aspiration for culture. CT scan of the neck with contrast is essential when deep-space extension, tracheal compression, mediastinal involvement, or retropharyngeal spread is suspected.

Fine-needle aspiration (FNA) of the abscess or inflammatory focus with Gram stain, aerobic, anaerobic, mycobacterial, and fungal cultures is the definitive microbiological test. Empirical antibiotics should begin before culture results are available if systemic sepsis is present.

For recurrent acute thyroiditis in children, barium or Gastrografin swallow under fluoroscopy detects a pyriform sinus fistula in approximately 50-80% of cases; CT or MRI neck may show the fistula tract when the swallow study is negative.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies abscess formation, hypoechoic inflammatory zone, guides aspiration, and assesses extent of involvement within the thyroid and adjacent structures
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Cool gel is applied to the neck and a probe gently scans the thyroid region. No radiation, no pain. Takes 10-20 minutes. Detects whether pus is present and guides needle placement for aspiration if needed.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Evaluates deep-space extension, retropharyngeal and mediastinal spread, airway compression, and identifies pyriform sinus fistula tract anatomy
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: 5-10 minutes in a CT scanner with contrast dye injected into a vein. A mild flushing sensation is normal. Essential for surgical planning when ultrasound shows a large or deep abscess, airway compromise, or clinical deterioration.

diagnosticTests.test: Fine-needle aspiration (FNA) of abscess with culture
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies causative organism, guides antibiotic selection, and can provide therapeutic drainage of a small abscess
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: After skin cleaning and local anesthetic, a fine needle is passed into the abscess using ultrasound guidance. Mild discomfort. The aspirate is submitted for Gram stain and culture (aerobic, anaerobic, fungal, mycobacterial). Gram stain in 24 hours; culture results in 2-5 days.

diagnosticTests.test: Thyroid function tests (TSH, free T4, free T3)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects transient thyrotoxicosis from follicle disruption and monitors for hypothyroidism from gland destruction in severe cases
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard blood draw. Results in 1-3 days. Most patients have normal or mildly abnormal function during acute thyroiditis; profound hypothyroidism suggests extensive gland damage.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies leukocytosis with neutrophilia consistent with bacterial infection; serial CRP monitors treatment response
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A routine blood draw; results in 1-2 hours. Expected findings: WBC above 12,000/µL with over 75% neutrophils; CRP above 50 mg/L in moderate-to-severe infection.

diagnosticTests.test: Barium or Gastrografin swallow (fluoroscopy) in children
diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects pyriform sinus fistula as the source of recurrent thyroiditis in pediatric patients; performed after the acute episode has resolved
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A child swallows contrast fluid while X-ray images are taken of the throat and esophagus. Takes 20-30 minutes. Fistula appears as a thin channel from the piriform sinus toward the left thyroid. Sensitivity 50-80%; repeat after full resolution if negative during acute inflammation.

treatmentOverview:
Management of acute suppurative thyroiditis requires both systemic antibiotics and, in cases with abscess formation, surgical drainage or aspiration. There are no dedicated randomized controlled trials; management is based on observational series, expert consensus from the American Thyroid Association and American Association of Clinical Endocrinologists, and pediatric surgical guidelines.

Empirical intravenous antibiotic therapy begins as soon as blood and aspirate cultures are drawn. For community-acquired acute thyroiditis, IV ampicillin-sulbactam 3 g every 6 hours (covering staphylococci, streptococci, and anaerobes) or IV cefazolin 2 g every 8 hours plus metronidazole 500 mg every 8 hours provides adequate initial coverage. When MRSA is suspected (purulent material, prior MRSA, IV drug use), IV vancomycin 25-30 mg/kg/day is added. In immunocompromised patients, broader empirical coverage with IV piperacillin-tazobactam or meropenem is often appropriate pending culture. De-escalation to oral antibiotics after 48-72 hours of IV therapy and clinical improvement completes a total 14-21 day course for most cases.

Surgical drainage is the definitive treatment for thyroid abscess not controlled by antibiotics alone. Ultrasound-guided needle aspiration is adequate for small, accessible abscesses; formal open surgical drainage (hemithyroidectomy or partial lobectomy) is required for large, multi-loculated, or anatomically complex abscesses, particularly in children with pyriform sinus fistula where concurrent fistulectomy (when tissues are not inflamed) is definitive. Surgical drainage improves outcome significantly; untreated abscess carries risks of mediastinal extension, airway compromise, and sepsis.

For radiation thyroiditis following I-131 therapy, NSAIDs (ibuprofen 400-600 mg three times daily) or short-course oral prednisone 20-40 mg/day for 5-7 days provide symptomatic relief. Antibiotics are not indicated unless secondary bacterial superinfection is suspected.

In children with a first confirmed episode, the pyriform sinus fistula should be identified and definitively repaired by fistulectomy or chemocauterization (endoscopic trichloroacetic acid application) to prevent recurrence. Recurrence rates without fistula repair approach 50-70% in published pediatric series.

medicalTreatments.name: IV ampicillin-sulbactam (3 g every 6 hours)
medicalTreatments.description: Beta-lactam/beta-lactamase inhibitor combination covering MSSA, Streptococcus, and oral anaerobes. First-line empirical intravenous therapy for community-acquired acute thyroiditis where MRSA is not a concern. Step down to oral amoxicillin-clavulanate after clinical improvement.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: No RCT data specific to thyroiditis; consistent with IDSA SSTI guidelines and case series reporting resolution in majority of non-MRSA cases

medicalTreatments.name: IV vancomycin (25-30 mg/kg/day in divided doses) for MRSA
medicalTreatments.description: First-line IV therapy when MRSA is suspected: purulent presentation, prior MRSA history, IV drug use, or failure of beta-lactam therapy. Continued until patient stable and able to transition to oral TMP-SMX or doxycycline based on culture results.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Standard of care for MRSA infections; clinical success rates 80-90% in soft tissue infections; no thyroiditis-specific data

medicalTreatments.name: Ibuprofen (400-600 mg three times daily) for radiation thyroiditis
medicalTreatments.description: NSAID therapy reduces pain, swelling, and fever from radiation-induced follicular necrosis within 24-48 hours of initiation. First-line symptomatic treatment for I-131 or radiation beam-induced acute thyroiditis without infectious cause.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Symptomatic resolution in 7-14 days in the majority of radiation thyroiditis cases based on clinical practice and case series

medicalTreatments.name: Oral prednisone (20-40 mg/day for 5-7 days)
medicalTreatments.description: Short course corticosteroid for severe radiation thyroiditis unresponsive to NSAIDs, or for inflammatory thyroiditis with significant compressive symptoms. Tapers over 7-10 days to avoid rebound. Not used in infectious thyroiditis where it would impair bacterial clearance.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Rapid symptomatic improvement within 24-48 hours in radiation and non-infectious inflammatory thyroiditis based on expert consensus and case reports

medicalTreatments.name: IV piperacillin-tazobactam (4.5 g every 6-8 hours)
medicalTreatments.description: Broad-spectrum beta-lactam/beta-lactamase inhibitor for immunocompromised patients with acute thyroiditis where Gram-negative organisms and anaerobes are more likely. Covers Pseudomonas and extended-spectrum beta-lactamase producing organisms when relevant.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Broad-spectrum empirical coverage pending culture results; de-escalated once organism-specific data available

surgicalOptions.name: Ultrasound-guided needle aspiration of thyroid abscess
surgicalOptions.description: Using ultrasound for real-time needle guidance, the abscess cavity is aspirated under local anesthetic. Provides therapeutic drainage and material for culture simultaneously. Repeat aspiration required in 30-40% of cases for complete drainage.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Resolution of abscess in 70-80% of cases after one or two aspirations in small to moderate abscesses; larger or loculated collections typically require open drainage

surgicalOptions.name: Surgical drainage — partial thyroidectomy or hemithyroidectomy
surgicalOptions.description: Open surgical drainage through an anterior neck incision for large, complex, multi-loculated abscesses or those with anatomical danger (proximity to trachea or great vessels). Involves removal of infected thyroid tissue along with drainage of pus.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Resolution in approximately 95% of operated cases when excision is complete; hypothyroidism may result from extensive tissue removal, requiring lifelong levothyroxine

surgicalOptions.name: Pyriform sinus fistulectomy (children)
surgicalOptions.description: Complete excision of the fistula tract from the pyriform sinus to the thyroid, performed 4-8 weeks after the acute episode has fully resolved. Prevents recurrent thyroiditis. Chemocauterization with trichloroacetic acid under endoscopy is a less invasive alternative.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Fistulectomy prevents recurrence in over 90% of children; chemocauterization success 70-85% with one or more sessions

whySeeSpecialist: Endocrinology input is necessary for monitoring thyroid function (detecting transient thyrotoxicosis and late hypothyroidism), confirming the diagnosis biochemically, and planning post-treatment surveillance. Head and neck surgery or ENT specialist evaluation is mandatory when abscess formation is suspected or confirmed, when there is any sign of airway compromise, or when a pyriform sinus fistula is suspected in a child with recurrent episodes. Pediatric endocrinology and pediatric surgery collaboration is essential for managing children.
doctorSelectionGuide: For adult acute thyroiditis, an endocrinologist working alongside a head and neck surgeon in a center with ultrasound-guided aspiration capability is the appropriate team. For children with recurrent acute thyroiditis, referral to a pediatric surgical center experienced in pyriform sinus fistulectomy is essential to prevent further recurrences and long-term thyroid gland damage.
hospitalCriteria: Any patient with suspected thyroid abscess, airway compromise, or signs of sepsis requires hospital admission for IV antibiotics and surgical evaluation
Immunocompromised patients with acute thyroiditis — higher risk of atypical organisms, rapid progression, and inadequate oral absorption
Children with first or recurrent episode of acute thyroiditis for IV therapy, ultrasound-guided aspiration, and pediatric surgery evaluation
Evidence of deep-space infection or mediastinal extension on CT imaging — requires immediate surgical management
keyFacilities: Hospitals with head and neck surgery and ultrasound-guided intervention capability
Pediatric surgical centers experienced in pyriform sinus fistulectomy
Endocrinology units with dynamic thyroid function monitoring
Intensive care units for septic patients with airway risk
preventionStrategies: Identify and surgically repair pyriform sinus fistulas after a child's first confirmed episode of acute left-sided thyroiditis — fistulectomy prevents recurrence in over 90% of cases.
Treat bacteremia and distant infection foci promptly to reduce risk of hematogenous thyroid seeding in immunocompromised patients.
Maintain oral hygiene and receive prompt dental treatment for periapical abscesses or periodontitis to eliminate the odontogenic entry portal for thyroid infection.
Monitor for radiation thyroiditis in all patients receiving radioactive iodine therapy, with pre-treatment warning and standby NSAID prescription, particularly for large toxic goiters.
lifestyleModifications: Complete the full antibiotic course (typically 14-21 days) even after symptom resolution, to prevent partial treatment and abscess recurrence.
Avoid neck compression, tight collars, and strenuous Valsalva maneuvers during the acute phase, which increase pressure on the inflamed thyroid.

Follow up with the endocrinologist 4-8 weeks after resolution for thyroid function tests — transient hypothyroidism can emerge weeks after acute thyroiditis and may require temporary levothyroxine replacement.
In children, attend post-acute follow-up with the pediatric surgeon to plan pyriform sinus fistula investigation and repair timing.

recommended: Soft, cool foods and liquids during the acute phase to reduce discomfort with swallowing
High fluid intake to maintain hydration during febrile illness and support antibiotic distribution
High-protein foods after the acute phase to support immune reconstitution and tissue repair
avoid: Hot beverages and spicy foods that may exacerbate throat and neck discomfort during the acute inflamed phase
Alcohol during antibiotic therapy, particularly with metronidazole, which produces a disulfiram-like reaction

exerciseGuidelines: Complete rest is recommended during the acute febrile phase of thyroiditis. Light activity can resume once fever has resolved, neck swelling has reduced, and the antibiotic course is underway. Return to full activity, including contact sports in children, should await surgical clearance if fistulectomy is planned, typically 6-12 weeks after the acute episode.
dailyManagement: Take antibiotics at evenly spaced intervals to maintain consistent blood levels; food improves tolerability of amoxicillin-clavulanate but does not substantially alter absorption.
Apply a cool cloth or ice pack wrapped in a towel to the neck for 15-20 minutes several times daily to reduce local discomfort.
Monitor temperature twice daily and report worsening fever or new difficulty breathing to the treating team immediately.
Observe for signs of thyrotoxicosis — tremor, palpitations, heat intolerance — during the acute phase from follicle disruption; report these to your endocrinologist.
Keep follow-up appointments with both the endocrinology and surgical teams, as the two phases of management (acute infection, fistula repair in children) require coordinated planning.
prognosis: The prognosis of acute thyroiditis depends on the underlying cause, the speed of diagnosis and treatment, and the immune status of the patient. Bacterial acute thyroiditis treated promptly with appropriate antibiotics and surgical drainage resolves in 2-4 weeks in the majority of cases with full recovery of thyroid function. Permanent hypothyroidism occurs in fewer than 5% of treated cases where infection is contained without extensive glandular destruction. Radiation thyroiditis is invariably self-limiting over 2-4 weeks with supportive care and does not produce permanent thyroid damage when treated. In children, recurrent acute thyroiditis from pyriform sinus fistula can produce cumulative thyroid damage with each episode; definitive fistulectomy prevents recurrence and preserves long-term thyroid function in over 90% of cases. The most dangerous outcomes — mediastinitis, airway obstruction, and septic death — occur in untreated or delayed cases; mortality from established thyroid abscess complicated by mediastinal extension exceeds 15-20% in case reports.
recoveryTimeline: Fever resolves within 48-72 hours of IV antibiotics. Pain and neck swelling improve within 5-7 days. Full antibiotic course typically 14-21 days. Ultrasound confirmation of abscess resolution at 4-6 weeks. Thyroid function reassessment at 4-8 weeks post-acute episode. Pyriform sinus fistula repair in children planned 4-8 weeks after full resolution of inflammation.
complications: Mediastinal extension — thyroid abscess can rupture posteriorly into the superior mediastinum, causing descending necrotizing mediastinitis with mortality above 20-40% without emergency surgical debridement.

Airway compression — rapidly expanding abscess, particularly in the posterior thyroid or paratracheal space, compresses the trachea causing stridor and respiratory failure requiring immediate intubation and surgical drainage.
Transient thyrotoxicosis — acute follicle disruption releases preformed thyroid hormone producing palpitations, tremor, and anxiety in a minority of patients; typically self-limiting over 2-6 weeks.
Permanent hypothyroidism — large abscess or extensive surgical resection results in insufficient residual thyroid tissue for normal hormone production; requires lifelong levothyroxine replacement.

Recurrent infection from untreated pyriform sinus fistula — in children, repeated episodes progressively damage thyroid tissue; each untreated recurrence increases the risk of permanent hypothyroidism and scarring.
Septic thrombophlebitis of adjacent veins — extension of suppurative process to internal jugular vein produces Lemierre-like syndrome with septic pulmonary emboli and high morbidity.

supportResources.name: American Thyroid Association — Thyroiditis Patient Information
supportResources.description: ATA patient-facing resource covering the spectrum of thyroiditis including acute, subacute, and autoimmune forms with management overview.

supportResources.name: British Thyroid Foundation
supportResources.description: UK patient charity providing educational information and peer support for individuals with thyroid disorders.

confusedWithConditions.name: Subacute thyroiditis (de Quervain's)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Subacute thyroiditis (de Quervain's) is a viral or post-viral granulomatous inflammation with gradual onset over days to weeks, typically following an upper respiratory infection. It is not bacterial, does not require antibiotics, and presents with tender thyroid enlargement that migrates from lobe to lobe ('creeping' pattern). Acute thyroiditis is bacterial, has abrupt onset, and shows suppurative aspirate on FNA.

confusedWithConditions.name: Autoimmune thyroiditis (Hashimoto's)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Hashimoto's thyroiditis is a chronic autoimmune lymphocytic infiltration producing a firm, painless goiter and progressive hypothyroidism over years. It does not cause fever, acute neck pain, or abscess. Anti-TPO and anti-thyroglobulin antibodies are elevated in Hashimoto's; acute thyroiditis is antibody-negative and produces leukocytosis.

confusedWithConditions.name: Thyroiditis (subacute and chronic forms)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: The various forms of thyroiditis — subacute, autoimmune, drug-induced, and radiation — differ fundamentally from acute suppurative thyroiditis in that they are not caused by bacterial infection and do not produce suppurative aspirate, bacteremia, or respond to antibiotics. Clinical distinction is critical because unnecessary antibiotics in non-bacterial thyroiditis delay correct treatment.

confusedWithConditions.name: Goiter
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Goiter is a non-inflammatory enlargement of the thyroid gland from iodine deficiency, multinodular hyperplasia, or Graves' disease. It is painless, non-tender, and does not produce fever or systemic infection. A suddenly painful goiter should raise suspicion for hemorrhage into a nodule or, in the context of fever, acute thyroiditis.

relatedConditions.name: Autoimmune thyroiditis (Hashimoto's)
relatedConditions.relevance: The most common form of thyroiditis with which acute thyroiditis must be distinguished; both produce thyroid enlargement and may produce hypothyroidism but through entirely different mechanisms.

relatedConditions.name: Subacute thyroiditis
relatedConditions.relevance: A common viral-triggered thyroiditis that is the primary diagnostic alternative to acute thyroiditis in a patient with a painful tender thyroid; distinguished by viral prodrome, lack of suppurative aspirate, and no response to antibiotics.

relatedConditions.name: Hypothyroidism
relatedConditions.relevance: May develop as a late consequence of severe acute thyroiditis with extensive glandular destruction; requires lifelong levothyroxine replacement.

relatedConditions.name: Hyperthyroidism
relatedConditions.relevance: Transient thyrotoxicosis from follicle disruption in acute thyroiditis can mimic hyperthyroidism; distinguished by the acute infectious context, fever, and normal or reduced radioiodine uptake.

faqs.question: What is acute thyroiditis?
faqs.answer: Acute thyroiditis is a rare bacterial infection of the thyroid gland causing sudden onset of severe anterior neck pain, fever, and a tender swollen thyroid. It accounts for fewer than 1% of thyroid disease. In children, a congenital fistula from the throat to the thyroid is the most common cause.

faqs.question: What causes acute thyroiditis?
faqs.answer: The most common cause is bacterial infection — Staphylococcus aureus and Streptococcus are the most frequent pathogens. In children, a congenital pyriform sinus fistula allows bacteria from the throat to reach the left thyroid lobe. In adults, bacteria usually reach the thyroid via the bloodstream, particularly in immunocompromised patients.

faqs.question: How is acute thyroiditis treated?
faqs.answer: Treatment combines intravenous antibiotics (ampicillin-sulbactam or cefazolin plus metronidazole) with surgical drainage of any abscess. In children, the pyriform sinus fistula must be repaired after recovery to prevent recurrence. Radiation thyroiditis is managed with NSAIDs, not antibiotics.

faqs.question: Is acute thyroiditis serious?
faqs.answer: Yes. Untreated thyroid abscess can compress the airway causing respiratory failure, or rupture into the mediastinum causing life-threatening mediastinitis. Treated promptly, most patients recover fully. Any patient with neck pain, fever, and thyroid swelling should seek same-day medical evaluation.

faqs.question: How long does acute thyroiditis last?
faqs.answer: With appropriate IV antibiotics and drainage, fever resolves in 48-72 hours and neck pain improves within 5-7 days. A full antibiotic course of 14-21 days is required. Ultrasound confirms abscess resolution at 4-6 weeks. Thyroid function returns to normal in most patients within 1-3 months.

faqs.question: What is a pyriform sinus fistula and why does it cause thyroiditis in children?
faqs.answer: A pyriform sinus fistula is a congenital channel connecting the pyriform sinus in the throat to the left thyroid lobe. It forms from incomplete closure of the third or fourth pharyngeal pouch during fetal development. Bacteria from the throat travel through this fistula into the thyroid, causing recurrent left-sided thyroid abscesses in young children.

faqs.question: Can acute thyroiditis cause hypothyroidism?
faqs.answer: Permanent hypothyroidism occurs in fewer than 5% of treated cases. Transient hypothyroidism can appear 4-8 weeks after the acute episode as the gland recovers. Thyroid function should be checked 4-8 weeks post-treatment and every 3-6 months for the first year to monitor for late hypothyroidism.

faqs.question: What is radiation thyroiditis?
faqs.answer: Radiation thyroiditis is acute thyroid inflammation occurring 5-14 days after radioactive iodine-131 therapy, caused by radiation damage to thyroid follicles. It presents with neck pain and tenderness but no fever and is not an infection. It resolves spontaneously over 2-4 weeks and is treated with ibuprofen or short-course steroids.

faqs.question: How do doctors diagnose acute thyroiditis?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis is clinical, supported by ultrasound (showing abscess formation), blood tests (leukocytosis, elevated CRP), and fine-needle aspiration culture confirming bacterial infection. CT neck is used when deep-space extension is suspected. Thyroid function tests are checked to detect transient hormone release.

faqs.question: Can acute thyroiditis recur?
faqs.answer: In children with an unrepaired pyriform sinus fistula, recurrence rates are 50-70%. Once the fistula is surgically repaired or treated by endoscopic chemocauterization, recurrence is prevented in over 90% of cases. Recurrence in adults is rare unless the underlying risk factor — immunosuppression or bacteremia source — persists.

faqs.question: What is the difference between acute thyroiditis and Hashimoto's disease?
faqs.answer: Hashimoto's thyroiditis is a chronic autoimmune condition with gradual painless thyroid enlargement and progressive hypothyroidism. Acute thyroiditis is a bacterial infection with sudden severe neck pain, fever, and abscess. Hashimoto's has elevated anti-TPO antibodies; acute thyroiditis has neutrophil leukocytosis and bacteria on culture.

faqs.question: Is acute thyroiditis common in children?
faqs.answer: Acute suppurative thyroiditis is rare overall, but among children it is the most common indication for thyroid surgery. It typically presents in children under 10 with a congenital pyriform sinus fistula, producing recurrent left-sided thyroid abscess. Prompt diagnosis and fistula repair prevent further episodes.

faqs.question: What organisms cause acute thyroiditis?
faqs.answer: Staphylococcus aureus and group A Streptococcus cause most cases in immunocompetent patients. Mixed anaerobes predominate in dental-source cases. In immunocompromised patients, Gram-negative bacilli, Aspergillus, Candida, and mycobacteria are additional possibilities and require broad empirical antibiotic coverage.

faqs.question: When should I go to the ER for neck pain and thyroid swelling?
faqs.answer: Seek emergency care for neck pain with high fever (above 39°C), difficulty breathing, stridor, rapid worsening swelling, or any difficulty swallowing, as these suggest an expanding thyroid abscess with airway risk. These are potentially life-threatening findings requiring immediate evaluation.

faqs.question: Can immunocompromised patients get unusual forms of acute thyroiditis?
faqs.answer: Yes. Patients with HIV-AIDS, hematologic malignancies, or post-transplant immunosuppression are susceptible to thyroid infection by unusual organisms including Aspergillus, Candida, Pneumocystis jirovecii, and atypical mycobacteria. These require aspiration for culture and specialized antifungal or antimycobacterial treatment.

metaDescription: Acute thyroiditis: bacterial thyroid infection causing severe neck pain, fever, and abscess. Causes, diagnosis, antibiotic and surgical treatment reviewed here.
